title: Christian - Jesus Christ
symbols: Logos in Judaism - Jewish Wisdom traditions
Gnostic Christ - Christ as revealer of gnosis
Gospel of Thomas - Alternative Jesus sayings
Isa in Islam - Jesus honored in Islam

primarySources.title: The Holy Bible
primarySources.author: Various (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, Paul, etc.)
primarySources.text: The primary source for the life, teachings, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. The Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John) provide narratives of his ministry, while the Epistles (letters) offer theological interpretations.

primarySources.title: Gospel of Thomas
primarySources.author: Unknown
primarySources.text: A non-canonical gospel consisting of a collection of sayings attributed to Jesus. While its historical accuracy is debated, it offers an alternative perspective on his teachings.

primarySources.title: The Nag Hammadi Library
primarySources.author: Various Gnostic Authors
primarySources.text: A collection of Gnostic texts discovered in Nag Hammadi, Egypt, which contain alternative interpretations of Jesus and his message, often emphasizing esoteric knowledge (gnosis).

primarySources.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews
primarySources.author: Flavius Josephus
primarySources.text: A first-century Jewish historian who mentions Jesus in his writings. While the authenticity of the 'Testimonium Flavianum' is debated, it is still a significant historical reference to Jesus outside of the Bible.

primarySources.title: Tacitus, Annals
primarySources.author: Tacitus
primarySources.text: A Roman historian who refers to Christ and his execution by Pontius Pilate in his account of the Great Fire of Rome. This provides an independent Roman perspective on the existence of Jesus and the early Christian movement.

name: Jesus Christ
description:
Jesus Christ is the central figure in Christianity, revered as the Son of God, the Messiah prophesied in the Old Testament (Tanakh), and the embodiment of divine love and salvation. His life, death, and resurrection form the cornerstone of Christian theology and belief. The narrative of Jesus begins with his miraculous birth in Bethlehem, to Mary, a virgin, and Joseph, a carpenter, as recounted in the Gospels of Matthew and Luke (Matthew 1:18-25; Luke 1:26-38; Luke 2:1-20). This event is celebrated annually as Christmas, marking the Incarnation, the moment God took on human form. According to the Gospels, his birth was heralded by angels and visited by magi bearing gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh, symbolizing his kingship, divinity, and eventual sacrifice. The infancy narratives highlight the divine nature of Jesus and his destined role as the savior of humanity. The star of Bethlehem, traditionally associated with his birth, guided the Magi to the location of the infant Jesus, demonstrating the cosmic significance of his arrival.

Jesus’s early life is sparsely documented, with the Gospels providing limited details about his childhood and adolescence. Luke's Gospel recounts a story of Jesus, at the age of twelve, visiting the Temple in Jerusalem and engaging in theological discussions with the religious leaders (Luke 2:41-52), showcasing his early wisdom and understanding of scripture. This event foreshadows his later role as a teacher and interpreter of divine law. The Gospels then remain silent until the commencement of Jesus’s public ministry around the age of thirty.

Jesus’s public ministry began after being baptized by John the Baptist in the Jordan River (Matthew 3:13-17; Mark 1:9-11; Luke 3:21-22). This event, marked by the descent of the Holy Spirit in the form of a dove and a voice from heaven proclaiming Jesus as God's Son, signified the commencement of his messianic mission. Following his baptism, Jesus retreated to the desert for forty days, where he was tempted by Satan (Matthew 4:1-11; Mark 1:12-13; Luke 4:1-13). These temptations, focusing on earthly power, material possessions, and testing God's protection, highlight Jesus's unwavering commitment to his divine purpose and rejection of worldly temptations.

After his time in the desert, Jesus returned to Galilee and began to preach the Kingdom of God, proclaiming, "The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel" (Mark 1:15). The Gospels portray Jesus as a charismatic teacher, healer, and miracle worker. He traveled throughout Galilee and Judea, preaching messages of love, forgiveness, and justice. His teachings often took the form of parables, short stories with moral or spiritual lessons, such as the Parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37), which emphasizes the importance of compassion and selfless service to others, and the Parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:11-32), which illustrates the boundless compassion of God and the importance of repentance and forgiveness. The Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7) is a central collection of Jesus’s ethical teachings, including the Beatitudes, which offer blessings to those who embody qualities of humility, meekness, and righteousness. These teachings challenge conventional societal values and advocate for a radical ethic of love and compassion.

Jesus performed numerous miracles, demonstrating his divine power and compassion. He healed the sick, cast out demons, calmed storms, and even raised the dead. These miracles served as signs of the coming Kingdom of God and affirmed his identity as the Messiah. Notable miracles include the healing of the paralytic (Matthew 9:1-8; Mark 2:1-12; Luke 5:17-26), demonstrating his power to forgive sins and heal physical ailments; the feeding of the five thousand (Matthew 14:13-21; Mark 6:30-44; Luke 9:10-17; John 6:1-14), showcasing his ability to provide for the needs of his followers; and the raising of Lazarus from the dead (John 11:1-44), a powerful demonstration of his power over death and a foreshadowing of his own resurrection. These acts of power and compassion drew large crowds and solidified Jesus’s reputation as a healer and miracle worker, but also aroused the suspicion and opposition of religious authorities.

Jesus gathered a group of twelve disciples, who became his closest followers and were commissioned to continue his ministry after his departure. The disciples included Simon Peter, James, John, Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Thomas, Matthew, James the Less, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot. The Gospels depict the disciples as ordinary individuals who were called to an extraordinary purpose. They accompanied Jesus on his travels, witnessed his miracles, and listened to his teachings. While they often struggled to understand the full implications of Jesus’s message and displayed moments of doubt and misunderstanding, they remained loyal to him and played a crucial role in spreading the Christian faith after his death and resurrection. Peter, James, and John are often considered to be the inner circle of disciples, witnessing key events such as the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-9; Mark 9:2-10; Luke 9:28-36).

Central to Christian theology is the concept of the Trinity: God as Father, Son (Jesus Christ), and Holy Spirit. Jesus, as the Son, is considered to be fully divine and fully human, a concept known as the Hypostatic Union. This doctrine, developed over centuries of theological reflection and debated in various ecumenical councils, seeks to reconcile the seemingly contradictory claims of Jesus’s divinity and humanity. The Nicene Creed, formulated in the fourth century at the Council of Nicaea, affirms the divinity of Jesus, stating that he is “God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God, begotten, not made, of one Being with the Father.” This creed became a foundational statement of Christian belief, solidifying the orthodox understanding of Jesus's identity.

The final week of Jesus’s life, known as Holy Week, is a pivotal period in Christian tradition. It begins with Jesus’s triumphal entry into Jerusalem (Matthew 21:1-11; Mark 11:1-11; Luke 19:28-44; John 12:12-19), where he was hailed as the Messiah by crowds waving palm branches and shouting "Hosanna!" This event is commemorated on Palm Sunday. During this week, Jesus shared a final meal with his disciples, known as the Last Supper (Matthew 26:17-30; Mark 14:12-26; Luke 22:7-38; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26), during which he instituted the Eucharist, also known as Holy Communion, as a memorial of his sacrifice. He also predicted his betrayal by Judas Iscariot and his own death. Following the Last Supper, Jesus went to the Garden of Gethsemane (Matthew 26:36-46; Mark 14:32-42; Luke 22:39-46), where he prayed in anguish, knowing that his crucifixion was imminent. His prayer in Gethsemane, "Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done" (Luke 22:42), demonstrates his obedience to God's will, even in the face of immense suffering.

Jesus was betrayed by Judas Iscariot and arrested by the authorities. He was subjected to a series of trials before the Jewish Sanhedrin and the Roman governor Pontius Pilate. Despite finding no fault in him, Pilate yielded to the pressure of the crowd and condemned Jesus to death by crucifixion. Jesus was crucified on a cross at Golgotha, also known as Calvary. His crucifixion is commemorated on Good Friday, a day of mourning and reflection for Christians. According to the Gospels, Jesus spoke several words from the cross, including “Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do” (Luke 23:34), demonstrating his compassion and forgiveness even to his executioners, and “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matthew 27:46; Mark 15:34), expressing his suffering and his unwavering faith in God. The inscription "Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews" (INRI) was placed above his head on the cross, highlighting the political implications of his messianic claims.

After his death, Jesus’s body was taken down from the cross and placed in a tomb. On the third day, Sunday, the tomb was found empty (Matthew 28:1-10; Mark 16:1-8; Luke 24:1-12; John 20:1-18). According to the Gospels, Jesus had risen from the dead, fulfilling his own prophecies and demonstrating his victory over death. The resurrection of Jesus is the central event in Christian faith, affirming his divinity and offering the hope of eternal life to believers. The resurrected Jesus appeared to his disciples and others, providing evidence of his resurrection and commissioning them to spread the Gospel to all nations. The Great Commission, as it is known, instructs the disciples to "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you" (Matthew 28:19-20). The resurrection is celebrated on Easter Sunday, a day of joy and celebration for Christians.

Forty days after his resurrection, Jesus ascended into heaven (Acts 1:6-11), marking the end of his earthly ministry and his return to the Father. The ascension signifies Jesus’s exaltation to the right hand of God and his ongoing intercession for believers. The Christian faith anticipates Jesus’s second coming, when he will return to judge the living and the dead and establish his eternal kingdom. This belief in the Parousia, or second coming, shapes Christian eschatology and informs their understanding of the end times.

The symbolism associated with Jesus Christ is rich and multifaceted. The cross is the most recognizable symbol of Christianity, representing Jesus’s sacrifice and the redemption of humanity. The lamb symbolizes Jesus as the sacrificial Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world (John 1:29), alluding to the Passover lamb in Jewish tradition. The fish (Ichthys) is an early Christian symbol, an acronym for “Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior.” The Sacred Heart represents Jesus’s divine love and compassion. Alpha and Omega symbolize Jesus as the beginning and the end, the eternal and unchanging God (Revelation 22:13). The dove represents the Holy Spirit, which descended upon Jesus at his baptism (Matthew 3:16; Mark 1:10; Luke 3:22; John 1:32). These symbols serve as visual reminders of the core beliefs and values of Christianity.

The cultural significance of Jesus Christ is immense and far-reaching. Christianity, based on his teachings and example, has had a profound impact on Western civilization, shaping its art, literature, music, philosophy, and ethics. Christian values, such as love, compassion, forgiveness, and justice, have influenced social reform movements and humanitarian efforts throughout history. Jesus’s teachings have inspired countless individuals to live lives of service and self-sacrifice. His message of hope and redemption continues to resonate with people around the world, making him one of the most influential figures in human history. Jesus is also an important figure in Islam, where he is known as Isa. Muslims revere Jesus as a prophet of God, but they do not believe that he is divine. The Quran affirms Jesus's miraculous birth and his performance of miracles, but it denies that he was crucified. Instead, Muslims believe that God raised Jesus to himself. Jesus is expected to return to earth before the Day of Judgment to restore justice and peace. His role as a significant prophet is also acknowledged in the Baha'i Faith.

The image of Jesus has been interpreted and represented in diverse ways throughout history, reflecting different cultural and theological perspectives. Early Christian art often depicted Jesus as the Good Shepherd, a symbol of care and protection. In the Byzantine era, Jesus was portrayed as the Pantocrator, the ruler of all, emphasizing his divine power and majesty. Renaissance artists, such as Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, created iconic depictions of Jesus that continue to shape popular perceptions of him. Contemporary artists continue to explore the meaning and significance of Jesus in their work, reflecting the ongoing relevance of his story and message. These artistic representations reflect the evolving understanding and interpretation of Jesus's identity and role throughout history.

In summary, Jesus Christ is the central figure of Christianity, embodying divine love, sacrifice, and redemption. His life, teachings, death, and resurrection form the foundation of Christian belief and have profoundly influenced Western civilization and beyond. He is revered as the Son of God, the Messiah, and the savior of humanity, whose message of hope and compassion continues to inspire and transform lives. His influence extends beyond Christianity, impacting other religions and cultures around the world.

summary: Also known as 🕊️ Jesus Christ. Associated with Christ, Messiah, Son of God, Son of Man, Lamb of God. Known as Christ. Symbolized by cross. His life, teachings, death, and resurrection form the foundation of Christian belief and have profoundly influenced Western civilization and beyond. He is revered as the Son of God, the Messiah, and the savior of humanity, whose message of hope and compassion continues to inspire and transform lives.
